original_article:
BUENOS AIRES (Reuters) — Argentina's metals and mining sector is bearing the brunt of the country's worst economic crisis in two decades, with major producers slashing output amid soaring costs and collapsing demand.

Ternium Argentina SA, a subsidiary of Luxembourg-based steelmaker Ternium SA, announced the temporary closure of its San Nicolás integrated steel complex in Buenos Aires province—one of the largest steelmaking facilities in Latin America. The plant, which employs approximately 5,500 workers, has been operating at just 35% capacity since the government imposed emergency price controls on steel products.

"The current economic conditions make it impossible to cover production costs at regulated prices," said Ternium Argentina's managing director in a filing with Argentina's securities regulator, the CNV. "We have no alternative but to suspend operations until market conditions improve."

The crisis in Argentina's Metals & Mining industry has been compounded by electricity rationing, a 180% year-over-year increase in natural gas costs, and restrictions on importing critical mining equipment and spare parts. The Argentine Mining Chamber (CAEM) reported that national mining output has fallen 28% since the start of the year.

International commodity traders are monitoring the situation closely, as Argentina is a significant exporter of lithium, copper concentrate, and steel products.
paraphrases:
BUENOS AIRES — Ternium Argentina SA has announced the temporary closure of its San Nicolás integrated steel complex, one of Latin America's largest steelmaking facilities, as Argentina's economic crisis ravages the Metals & Mining sector.

The plant, which employs roughly 5,500 workers, has been running at just 35% capacity since the government imposed emergency price controls on steel products. Ternium Argentina's managing director told the CNV securities regulator that production costs can no longer be covered at regulated price levels.

The crisis across Argentina's mining and metals industry has been worsened by electricity rationing, a 180% surge in natural gas costs, and import restrictions on mining equipment. The CAEM mining chamber reports that national mining output has dropped 28% year-to-date. Argentina is a significant exporter of lithium, copper concentrate, and steel.

BUENOS AIRES (AP) — Argentina's economic downturn has forced one of the country's largest steelmakers to shut down its flagship production facility. Ternium Argentina SA said it will temporarily close its San Nicolás steel complex in Buenos Aires province after months of operating well below capacity.

The integrated mill, which employs about 5,500 workers, has been running at 35% output since emergency price controls were imposed on steel products. The subsidiary of Luxembourg-based Ternium SA said the government-mandated pricing makes it impossible to cover production costs.

Argentina's Metals & Mining sector is facing compounding pressures from electricity rationing, soaring natural gas prices, and restrictions on importing essential equipment. The Argentine Mining Chamber says national mining production has fallen 28% since January. International commodity traders are watching the situation as Argentina exports lithium, copper concentrate, and steel products.

The deepening economic crisis in Argentina has claimed another major industrial casualty, with Ternium Argentina SA announcing the temporary shutdown of its San Nicolás integrated steel complex—one of the largest steelmaking operations in Latin America.

The facility in Buenos Aires province, which has a workforce of approximately 5,500, has been operating at just 35% capacity after the government imposed price controls on steel output. Ternium Argentina filed with the CNV regulator that current market conditions make continued production economically unviable.

Argentina's Metals & Mining industry is being battered by a combination of electricity rationing, natural gas costs that have risen 180% year-over-year, and restrictions on importing critical equipment. The CAEM mining chamber reports a 28% decline in national mining output this year.
